config.title: Applied SQL Fundamentals for Finance
config.description: Learn SQL fundamentals through practical finance analytics tasks. Students query account, balance, risk, interest, and advisor data while building job-ready SQL skills for finance, fintech, banking, and business analytics roles.
learnOutcomes: Retrieve finance records using SELECT statements.
Filter account data by status, balance, region, type, and missing values.
Sort and limit financial records for reporting and review.
Create readable financial outputs using aliases, calculations, string functions, date functions, and CASE logic.
Aggregate finance data to calculate portfolio summaries by category.
Apply SQL fundamentals to realistic finance analyst workflows.

questions.sectionDescription: Start by retrieving finance account records and shaping outputs for stakeholders.

lesson.title: Finance Account Dataset
lesson.body:
You are working as a junior finance analyst. Your team manages customer accounts across savings, current, investment, and loan products.

You will use the finance_accounts table. It contains account balances, product types, regions, account dates, risk ratings, account status, and assigned advisors.

Your job is to turn business questions into SQL queries that a finance team could use for reporting, review, and decision-making.

questions.question: Select one column. The client reporting team needs a simple list of customer names. Return only the `customer_name` column from `finance_accounts`.

lesson.title: Select One Column
lesson.body: The most basic finance query retrieves one field from a table. In reporting work, this is useful when you need a quick customer, account, advisor, or product list.

sqlTables.seedSql:
CREATE TABLE finance_accounts (
  account_id INTEGER,
  customer_name VARCHAR,
  account_type VARCHAR,
  region VARCHAR,
  opened_date DATE,
  closed_date DATE,
  balance INTEGER,
  interest_rate DOUBLE,
  risk_rating VARCHAR,
  status VARCHAR,
  advisor VARCHAR
);

INSERT INTO finance_accounts VALUES
  (1, 'Ada Okafor', 'Savings', 'Lagos', '2026-01-05', NULL, 85000, 0.035, 'Low', 'Active', 'Ngozi'),
  (2, 'Kojo Mensah', 'Investment', 'Accra', '2025-11-12', NULL, 250000, 0.075, 'Medium', 'Active', 'Mensah'),
  (3, 'Amina Bello', 'Loan', 'Abuja', '2025-08-21', NULL, -120000, 0.14, 'High', 'Active', 'Chidi'),
  (4, 'Tunde Balogun', 'Current', 'Lagos', '2026-02-10', NULL, 45000, 0.0, 'Low', 'Active', 'Ngozi'),
  (5, 'Grace Njeri', 'Investment', 'Nairobi', '2025-12-01', NULL, 320000, 0.082, 'Medium', 'Active', 'Achieng'),
  (6, 'Fatima Yusuf', 'Savings', 'Kano', '2026-03-15', NULL, 150000, 0.04, 'Low', 'Active', 'Chidi'),
  (7, 'Samuel Otieno', 'Loan', 'Nairobi', '2024-07-20', '2026-01-31', 0, 0.13, 'Medium', 'Closed', 'Achieng'),
  (8, 'Zainab Musa', 'Savings', 'Abuja', '2026-04-02', NULL, 60000, 0.035, 'Low', 'Active', 'Chidi');

sqlHints: The table is `finance_accounts`.

Your SELECT list should include only one column:
- customer_name
Do not include account_id, balance, or any other column.

questions.question: Select multiple columns. The portfolio review team wants to see the customer, account product, and balance for each account. Return `customer_name`, `account_type`, and `balance`.

lesson.title: Select Multiple Columns
lesson.body: Finance reports usually need several fields together: who the account belongs to, what product it is, and what value is attached to it.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT
  customer_name,
  _ _ _,
  _ _ _
FROM finance_accounts;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  customer_name,
  account_type,
  balance
FROM finance_accounts;
sqlHints: Use the same table: `finance_accounts`.

The output should include:
- customer_name
- account_type
- balance
Keep the columns in the requested order.

questions.question: Select top rows. The finance manager wants a quick preview of the account table. Return all columns for the first 3 rows only.

lesson.title: Select Top Rows
lesson.body: Before writing a full analysis, analysts often preview a small number of rows to understand the table structure and data quality.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
-- Preview the first few account records
SELECT *
FROM finance_accounts
LIMIT _ _ _;
sqlHints: Use `SELECT *` when you want to inspect every column.
Use LIMIT to control how many rows are returned.
The manager asked for exactly 3 rows.

questions.question: Rename columns using aliases. The reporting dashboard needs clearer column labels. Return `customer_name` as `client`, `account_type` as `product`, and `balance` as `current_balance`.

lesson.title: Rename Columns With Aliases
lesson.body: Aliases make SQL output easier for business users to read. In finance reporting, clear names reduce confusion when results are exported to dashboards or spreadsheets.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT
  customer_name AS _ _ _,
  account_type AS _ _ _,
  balance AS _ _ _
FROM finance_accounts;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  customer_name AS client,
  account_type AS product,
  balance AS current_balance
FROM finance_accounts;
sqlHints: Use `AS` after each selected column.

The required aliases are:
- client
- product
- current_balance
Column aliases affect the result headers, so spelling matters.

questions.question: Remove duplicate rows. The product strategy team wants to know which account products exist. Return the unique `account_type` values from the table.

lesson.title: Remove Duplicates With DISTINCT
lesson.body: DISTINCT removes duplicate values. This is useful for checking product types, regions, advisors, currencies, channels, or risk categories.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT _ _ _ account_type
FROM finance_accounts;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT DISTINCT account_type
FROM finance_accounts;
sqlHints: You need only one column: account_type.
Use DISTINCT immediately after SELECT.
The result should list each account type once.

questions.sectionDescription: Use WHERE conditions to answer targeted finance questions.

questions.question: Filter by single condition. The account operations team wants only active accounts. Return `account_id`, `customer_name`, and `status` where `status` is `Active`.

lesson.title: Filter by Single Condition
lesson.body: A single WHERE condition lets you focus a report on one business state, such as active accounts, completed payments, approved loans, or open invoices.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT
  account_id,
  customer_name,
  status
FROM finance_accounts
WHERE _ _ _ = 'Active';
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  account_id,
  customer_name,
  status
FROM finance_accounts
WHERE status = 'Active';
sqlHints: The condition belongs in the WHERE clause.
Filter the `status` column.
The value must be `Active`.

questions.question: Filter by multiple conditions. The Lagos investment team wants active investment accounts in Lagos. Return `account_id`, `customer_name`, `account_type`, and `region`.

lesson.title: Filter by Multiple Conditions
lesson.body: Finance questions often combine conditions: a product, a region, a status, a balance threshold, or a risk category. Use AND when all conditions must be true.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT
  account_id,
  customer_name,
  account_type,
  region
FROM finance_accounts
WHERE account_type = _ _ _
  AND region = _ _ _;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  account_id,
  customer_name,
  account_type,
  region
FROM finance_accounts
WHERE account_type = 'Investment'
  AND region = 'Lagos';
sqlHints: Use two WHERE conditions joined by AND.
The product condition is account_type = 'Investment'.
The region condition is region = 'Lagos'. This task may return no rows if no matching account exists.

questions.question: Filter by ranges. The relationship managers want accounts with balances between 50000 and 200000 inclusive. Return `customer_name`, `account_type`, and `balance`, ordered by `balance` ascending.

lesson.title: Filter by Ranges
lesson.body: Range filters are common in finance: balance bands, credit score ranges, transaction amount ranges, interest-rate ranges, and date ranges.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
-- Return accounts whose balance falls inside the requested range
SELECT
  customer_name,
  account_type,
  balance
FROM finance_accounts
WHERE balance BETWEEN _ _ _ AND _ _ _
ORDER BY balance ASC;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  customer_name,
  account_type,
  balance
FROM finance_accounts
WHERE balance BETWEEN 50000 AND 200000
ORDER BY balance ASC;
sqlHints: BETWEEN includes the lower and upper values.
The lower balance is 50000.
The upper balance is 200000.

questions.question: Filter by list membership. The product head wants all Savings and Investment accounts. Return `customer_name`, `account_type`, and `balance`, ordered by `account_type` then `customer_name`.

lesson.title: Filter by List Membership
lesson.body: Use IN when a filter can match several allowed values. This is useful for product groups, regions, risk categories, currencies, or transaction types.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT
  customer_name,
  account_type,
  balance
FROM finance_accounts
WHERE account_type IN (_ _ _, _ _ _)
ORDER BY account_type, customer_name;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  customer_name,
  account_type,
  balance
FROM finance_accounts
WHERE account_type IN ('Savings', 'Investment')
ORDER BY account_type, customer_name;
sqlHints: Use the IN operator.

The list should contain:
- 'Savings'
- 'Investment'
Sort first by account_type, then by customer_name.

questions.question: Filter by pattern matching. The client support team wants customers whose names start with `A`. Return `customer_name` and `region`, ordered by `customer_name`.

lesson.title: Filter by Pattern Matching
lesson.body: LIKE is useful when matching part of a text value. It can help with customer search, advisor names, descriptions, transaction references, or product labels.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT
  customer_name,
  region
FROM finance_accounts
WHERE customer_name LIKE _ _ _
ORDER BY customer_name;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  customer_name,
  region
FROM finance_accounts
WHERE customer_name LIKE 'A%'
ORDER BY customer_name;
sqlHints: Use LIKE for text pattern matching.
The percent sign means any characters after the first letter.
Names that start with A match the pattern 'A%'.

questions.question: Filter for missing values. Compliance wants accounts that have not been closed. Return `account_id`, `customer_name`, and `closed_date` where `closed_date` is missing.

lesson.title: Filter Missing Values
lesson.body: Missing values are represented as NULL. In finance data, missing close dates can mean an account is still open, while missing payment dates can mean an invoice has not been paid.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT
  account_id,
  customer_name,
  closed_date
FROM finance_accounts
WHERE closed_date _ _ _;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  account_id,
  customer_name,
  closed_date
FROM finance_accounts
WHERE closed_date IS NULL;
sqlHints: Do not use `= NULL`.
SQL checks missing values with IS NULL.
The missing field is closed_date.

questions.sectionDescription: Control the order and size of finance query outputs.

questions.question: Sort by single column. The finance director wants accounts listed from highest balance to lowest balance. Return `customer_name` and `balance`.

lesson.title: Sort by a Single Column
lesson.body: Sorting makes query results useful for review. Finance teams often sort by balance, exposure, interest rate, revenue, risk, or date.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT
  customer_name,
  balance
FROM finance_accounts
ORDER BY _ _ _ DESC;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  customer_name,
  balance
FROM finance_accounts
ORDER BY balance DESC;
sqlHints: The sort column is balance.
Highest to lowest requires DESC.
Only return customer_name and balance.

questions.question: Sort by multiple columns. The regional team wants accounts grouped by region, and within each region, highest balances first. Return `region`, `customer_name`, and `balance`.

lesson.title: Sort by Multiple Columns
lesson.body: Multi-column sorting is common in reports. You might sort by region first, then balance; or by advisor first, then risk rating; or by month first, then revenue.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
-- Sort by region first, then balance from high to low
SELECT
  region,
  customer_name,
  balance
FROM finance_accounts
ORDER BY _ _ _, _ _ _ DESC;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  region,
  customer_name,
  balance
FROM finance_accounts
ORDER BY region, balance DESC;
sqlHints: ORDER BY can accept more than one column.
The first sort should group rows by region.
The second sort should put higher balances first within each region.

questions.question: Specify sort direction. The onboarding team wants the newest accounts first. Return `customer_name`, `opened_date`, and `account_type`, ordered by `opened_date` descending.

lesson.title: Specify Sort Direction
lesson.body: ASC sorts from smallest to largest or oldest to newest. DESC sorts from largest to smallest or newest to oldest.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT
  customer_name,
  opened_date,
  account_type
FROM finance_accounts
ORDER BY opened_date _ _ _;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  customer_name,
  opened_date,
  account_type
FROM finance_accounts
ORDER BY opened_date DESC;
sqlHints: Newest dates come first when you sort descending.
Use ORDER BY opened_date DESC.
Return only the three requested columns.

questions.question: Limit number of rows returned. Risk leadership wants the 3 accounts with the highest balances. Return `customer_name`, `account_type`, and `balance`.

lesson.title: Limit Rows Returned
lesson.body: LIMIT is often used for top-N reporting, such as top customers, highest balances, largest transactions, or newest accounts.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
-- Find the top 3 balances
SELECT
  customer_name,
  account_type,
  balance
FROM finance_accounts
ORDER BY balance DESC
LIMIT _ _ _;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  customer_name,
  account_type,
  balance
FROM finance_accounts
ORDER BY balance DESC
LIMIT 3;
sqlHints: First sort balances from highest to lowest.
Then limit the output to 3 rows.
The result should show only the top accounts.

questions.sectionDescription: Create calculated fields and readable finance outputs.

questions.question: Perform arithmetic operations. The treasury team wants estimated annual interest for positive-balance accounts. Return `customer_name`, `balance`, `interest_rate`, and `estimated_annual_interest` as `balance * interest_rate`. Order by `estimated_annual_interest` descending.

lesson.title: Arithmetic in SQL
lesson.body: Finance analysis frequently requires calculated fields: interest, fees, margins, exposure, repayment amounts, revenue, variance, and growth rates.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT
  customer_name,
  balance,
  interest_rate,
  _ _ _ AS estimated_annual_interest
FROM finance_accounts
WHERE balance > 0
ORDER BY estimated_annual_interest DESC;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  customer_name,
  balance,
  interest_rate,
  balance * interest_rate AS estimated_annual_interest
FROM finance_accounts
WHERE balance > 0
ORDER BY estimated_annual_interest DESC;
sqlHints: The calculated field is balance multiplied by interest_rate.
Only positive balances should be included.
Alias the calculation as estimated_annual_interest.

questions.question: Apply string functions. The CRM export needs standardized text labels. Return `customer_name` as `client_name`, `upper(account_type)` as `product_label`, and `lower(region)` as `region_key`.

lesson.title: String Functions
lesson.body: String functions clean and standardize text fields. Analysts use them for customer names, product labels, transaction descriptions, region codes, and campaign tags.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT
  customer_name AS client_name,
  upper(_ _ _) AS product_label,
  lower(_ _ _) AS region_key
FROM finance_accounts;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  customer_name AS client_name,
  upper(account_type) AS product_label,
  lower(region) AS region_key
FROM finance_accounts;
sqlHints: Use upper() for product labels.
Use lower() for region keys.

Aliases should be:
- client_name
- product_label
- region_key

questions.question: Apply date/time functions. The onboarding team wants to group accounts by opening month. Return `account_id`, `customer_name`, and `opened_month` using `strftime(opened_date, '%Y-%m')`. Order by `account_id`.

lesson.title: Date Functions
lesson.body: Date functions help create monthly, quarterly, and yearly finance reports. They are useful for account openings, loan start dates, payment dates, invoice due dates, and transaction timestamps.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT
  account_id,
  customer_name,
  strftime(_ _ _, '%Y-%m') AS opened_month
FROM finance_accounts
ORDER BY account_id;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  account_id,
  customer_name,
  strftime(opened_date, '%Y-%m') AS opened_month
FROM finance_accounts
ORDER BY account_id;
sqlHints: The date column is opened_date.
Use the format '%Y-%m' to return year and month.
Sort by account_id to keep the output easy to verify.

questions.question: Apply conditional logic. The finance team wants to segment accounts by balance. Return `customer_name`, `balance`, and `balance_segment`. Use `High` for balances >= 200000, `Medium` for balances >= 50000, `Low` for balances >= 0, and `Debt` for negative balances. Order by `balance` descending.

lesson.title: Conditional Logic With CASE
lesson.body: CASE turns raw numbers into business labels. In finance, this is useful for value bands, risk categories, delinquency stages, profitability groups, and account health labels.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
-- Create a finance-friendly label for each balance
SELECT
  customer_name,
  balance,
  CASE
    WHEN balance >= _ _ _ THEN 'High'
    WHEN balance >= _ _ _ THEN 'Medium'
    WHEN balance >= _ _ _ THEN 'Low'
    ELSE 'Debt'
  END AS balance_segment
FROM finance_accounts
ORDER BY balance DESC;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  customer_name,
  balance,
  CASE
    WHEN balance >= 200000 THEN 'High'
    WHEN balance >= 50000 THEN 'Medium'
    WHEN balance >= 0 THEN 'Low'
    ELSE 'Debt'
  END AS balance_segment
FROM finance_accounts
ORDER BY balance DESC;
sqlHints: CASE checks conditions from top to bottom.

Use these thresholds:
- 200000 for High
- 50000 for Medium
- 0 for Low
Use ELSE for negative balances.

questions.sectionDescription: Summarize finance records into useful management metrics.

questions.question: Calculate column summaries. The CFO wants a quick summary of active account balances. Return `active_accounts`, `total_balance`, and `average_balance` for active accounts only.

lesson.title: Calculate Column Summaries
lesson.body: Aggregations summarize many records into a few metrics. Finance teams use them for total balances, average exposure, count of accounts, total revenue, and average loan size.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS active_accounts,
  SUM(_ _ _) AS total_balance,
  AVG(_ _ _) AS average_balance
FROM finance_accounts
WHERE status = 'Active';
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS active_accounts,
  SUM(balance) AS total_balance,
  AVG(balance) AS average_balance
FROM finance_accounts
WHERE status = 'Active';
sqlHints: Filter to active accounts first.
Use COUNT(*) for the number of active accounts.
Use SUM(balance) and AVG(balance) for the balance summaries.

questions.question: Group data by categories. Product leadership wants balance totals by account type. Return `account_type`, `account_count`, and `total_balance`, ordered by `total_balance` descending.

lesson.title: Group Data by Categories
lesson.body: GROUP BY creates category-level summaries. In finance, grouping by product, region, risk rating, advisor, or status is central to management reporting.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
SELECT
  account_type,
  COUNT(*) AS account_count,
  SUM(balance) AS total_balance
FROM finance_accounts
GROUP BY _ _ _
ORDER BY total_balance DESC;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  account_type,
  COUNT(*) AS account_count,
  SUM(balance) AS total_balance
FROM finance_accounts
GROUP BY account_type
ORDER BY total_balance DESC;
sqlHints: The category is account_type.
COUNT(*) gives the number of accounts in each product group.
SUM(balance) gives the total balance for each product group.

questions.question: Filter aggregated results. The finance director only wants product groups with total balances above 200000. Return `account_type` and `total_balance`, ordered by `total_balance` descending.

lesson.title: Filter Aggregated Results With HAVING
lesson.body: WHERE filters individual rows before aggregation. HAVING filters grouped results after aggregation. Use HAVING for questions like product groups above a balance threshold or regions with more than 100 accounts.

questions.sqlStarterCode:
-- Use HAVING because the filter depends on SUM(balance)
SELECT
  account_type,
  SUM(balance) AS total_balance
FROM finance_accounts
GROUP BY account_type
HAVING _ _ _ > 200000
ORDER BY total_balance DESC;
questions.sqlSolution:
SELECT
  account_type,
  SUM(balance) AS total_balance
FROM finance_accounts
GROUP BY account_type
HAVING SUM(balance) > 200000
ORDER BY total_balance DESC;
sqlHints: Use GROUP BY account_type first.
The aggregate filter should use SUM(balance).
Use HAVING, not WHERE, because the condition depends on the grouped total.
